---
title: "Usage"
---

# Usage

This section describes how to set up, build, flash, and use the Air Analyzer prototype.

The project is organized around a custom STM32-based embedded board designed to acquire pressure variations inside a wearable mask, store the raw data locally, and allow post-session analysis.

---

## Repository

Clone the project repository:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/AlessiaRinaldi/air_analyzer.git
cd air_analyzer
```

The repository contains the firmware source code, hardware design files, and project documentation.

---

## Firmware Project

The firmware is located in the `micro_usb` directory:

```text
micro_usb/
```

The main application logic is implemented in:

```text
micro_usb/Core/Src/main.c
```

The project was generated using STM32CubeMX. Peripheral initialization code is generated automatically, while the application-specific code is placed inside the dedicated `USER CODE BEGIN` and `USER CODE END` sections.

---

## Development Environment

The firmware can be opened and built using an STM32 development environment such as:

- STM32CubeIDE;
- Visual Studio Code with STM32 support;
- ARM GCC toolchain and make-based workflow.

During development, the board can be programmed and debugged through the STDC14 connector using an ST-Link interface.

---

## Build and Flash

To run the firmware on the board:

1. Open the firmware project inside the chosen development environment.
2. Build the project.
3. Connect the board to the programmer through the STDC14 debug connector.
4. Flash the firmware to the STM32L052 microcontroller.
5. Reset the board and start the acquisition.

During firmware development, the debug connector can also be used to power the board without relying on the CR2032 battery.

---

## Acquisition Workflow

Once the firmware is running, the device follows this acquisition sequence:

1. the STM32 initializes the configured peripherals;
2. the LPS22HBTR pressure sensor is configured for continuous acquisition at 10 Hz;
3. pressure data are acquired through SPI using DMA;
4. each pressure sample is stored in raw 24-bit format;
5. the external EEPROM stores the acquired data locally;
6. the data can be retrieved after the recording session for offline analysis.

The device stores only pressure data. Temperature data are not acquired or saved in the current firmware implementation.

---

## Stored Data Format

Each pressure sample occupies 3 bytes, corresponding to the native 24-bit pressure output of the LPS22HBTR sensor.

The stored byte order is:

```text
PRESS_OUT_XL
PRESS_OUT_L
PRESS_OUT_H
```

At a sampling rate of 10 Hz, the memory usage is:

```text
3 bytes/sample × 10 samples/s = 30 bytes/s
```

With a 32 KB EEPROM, the approximate maximum recording time is:

```text
32768 bytes / 30 bytes/s ≈ 1092 s ≈ 18 min
```

If a 64 KB memory is used, the expected recording time becomes approximately 36 minutes.

---

## Post-Processing

The raw pressure samples can be converted offline after data retrieval.

The 24-bit pressure value must be reconstructed from the three stored bytes and interpreted as a signed two's complement value. The pressure in hPa can then be obtained as:

```text
pressure_hPa = pressure_raw / 4096
```

Offline processing can be used to:

- reconstruct the pressure waveform;
- filter noise and motion artifacts;
- identify inhalation and exhalation phases;
- estimate breathing frequency;
- compare respiratory patterns between different users or exercise conditions.

---

## Current Limitations

The current prototype focuses on validating the embedded acquisition and storage pipeline. Some aspects are still under development:

- automatic data download from EEPROM through USB-C;
- complete post-processing software for respiratory-rate extraction;
- experimental validation during real physical activity;
- long-term power consumption measurements;
- mechanical optimization of the mask and PCB mounting.

---

## Future Work

Future improvements will focus on both firmware and system-level validation.

Possible developments include:

- implementing a complete USB-based data download tool;
- adding a structured memory management system for multiple recording sessions;
- improving the low-power strategy by using deeper sleep modes;
- validating the signal quality during different sports activities;
- comparing measurements between trained and untrained users;
- designing a more compact PCB revision;
- improving the mechanical integration of the electronics into the mask.

These improvements would make the system more robust, easier to use, and more suitable for experimental respiratory monitoring during real exercise sessions.
